Director's Profile

Planning, Sustainability & Special Projects Directorate

EA
Emil Mawuli Kofi Afenyo
Director, PSSP
Central University
🎯 Directorate Director

Emil Mawuli Kofi Afenyo

Director, Planning, Sustainability & Special Projects (PSSP) | Central University
Emil Mawuli Kofi Afenyo, BA (Hons), MSc (LSE), FGIM, is an accomplished higher education administrator, with over two decades of distinguished service at Central University. A graduate of the University of Ghana and the London School of Economics, Emil holds a Master of Science in Development Management — an academic foundation that has informed a career defined by institutional transformation, strategic vision, and operational excellence. As Director of the PSSP Directorate, Emil leads Central University's strategic planning, monitoring and evaluation, rationalisation, and sustainability agenda. He is the architect of the Phase 2 Rationalisation Programme — a bold institutional reform targeting GH¢9.34m in combined savings — and the principal designer of the University's Integrated M&E Plan 2025–2028. Before assuming the directorship of PSSP, Emil served as University Registrar from 2016, the most senior administrative post at Central University, where he served as Secretary to both the University Council and the Academic Board. In that role, he led a decade of institutional modernisation that reshaped how CU operates, learns, and grows.
✉️ [email protected]
📞 +233 030 331 8580 Ext. 201
📍 Admin Block, Miotso Campus
Highlights
Key Achievements

Authored "Landscape for Learning" (2007–2008), the visionary planning document that became the blueprint for the Miotso Permanent Campus and the School of Applied Sciences — transforming Central University's physical and academic footprint for generations.

As Registrar, facilitated a landmark GH¢5.5 million grant in partnership with the German Development Bank (KFW) and Stanbic Bank during the pandemic — financing 50% of all staff salaries for six months and protecting the livelihoods of every CU employee.

Spearheaded the construction of a 400kW peak solar system at Miotso — delivering annual savings exceeding GH¢400,000, reducing the University's grid dependency, and establishing CU as a leader in campus sustainability in Ghana.

Led the full implementation of OSIS (Online Student Information System), migrating records for 9,000 students, eliminating paper-based processing, and creating Ghana's first university-wide digital student management platform at CU.

Chaired the CODE Project — Central University's landmark initiative to move all teaching, learning, and examinations online during COVID-19. Successfully completed the full semester using Moodle LMS with zero academic year lost.

Recipient of the Best Worker Award (Senior Member Category, 2008). Redesigned CU's website to win the Ghana Web Awards 2002 Corporate Website of the Year. Draftsman of CU's Statutes and Charter (2010) — the foundational governance documents of the University.

Oversaw the refurbishment of the Student Plaza (creating 30+ offices, a Moot Law Court, and the Faculty of Law), six new air-conditioned classrooms from an abandoned basement, and a USD 60,000 ultra-modern data centre negotiated with ATC Towers.

Designed and leads the Phase 2 Programme Rationalisation — closing 14 underperforming programmes, targeting a 58% reduction in class sections, achieving campus specialisation across three sites, and unlocking GH¢9.34m in combined institutional savings by 2028.

Experience
Career at Central University

Leads strategic planning, institutional M&E, Phase 2 Rationalisation, and all sustainability initiatives. Architect of the Integrated M&E Plan 2025–2028 and the Phase 2 KPI Framework.

Head of Administration and Secretary to the University Council and Academic Board. Led COVID-19 response, GH¢5.5m grant facilitation, solar project, campus transformation, and major digital initiatives.

Led the Academic Directorate strategy, student records, and registry services. Secretary to the Academic Board.

Oversaw all Academic Registry operations; led student information management improvements.

Responsible for Admissions, Student Services, and Examinations. Implemented OSIS, created MyCUC student portal, and established the Student Services Centres at Miotso and Mataheko.

Established CU's Planning Unit; authored the Miotso Campus proposal; designed the first staff satisfaction survey; co-authored the MBA and Communication Studies programme documents.

Developed CU's first personnel database; designed staff appraisal systems; co-authored the Conditions of Service and wrote the CUC Matriculation Oath and Core Values document.

Designed and maintained CU's award-winning website — winner of the Ghana Web Awards 2002 Corporate Website of the Year.

✉️

Our Staff

The PSSP team driving planning, sustainability, and institutional efficiency at Central University

WN
📷 Change Photo

Ms. Winifred Narh Okletey

Administrative Assistant

Winifred Narh Okletey is a dedicated administrative professional who holds an MBA in Human Resource Management from Central University. She specializes in project management support, data analysis, and office logistics. Her key responsibilities include providing essential administrative assistance in the preparation of grant proposals and the management of special projects. Winifred is highly skilled in data and report handling, which she utilizes to support strategic directorate planning and institutional reporting.

Beyond office administration, she is an active member of the institutional Protocol Team. In this capacity, she supports logistics, decoration arrangements, and assists with guest relations for university functions and official events. 

✉️ [email protected]📞 +233 245 409 974
TL
📷 Change Photo

Ms. Theodora Eyram Ladi

Senior Assistant Registrar
A seasoned Human Resource Management professional with over 17 years of experience spanning strategic HR planning, policy formulation, talent management, and organisational development, Theodora brings deep institutional knowledge and a people-centred leadership philosophy to the PSSP Directorate. She holds a postgraduate qualification in Business Administration (HRM) from Open University Malaysia and a Bachelor's degree in Management Studies from the University of Cape Coast.
In her current role, Theodora contributes to strategic planning support, stakeholder engagement, and the coordination of special projects and sustainability initiatives. Her career spans senior HR leadership at Central University — where she oversaw a workforce of over 500 staff — as well as roles in international education liaison, child welfare, and healthcare administration across Ghana and the United Kingdom.
A member of the Institute of HR Practitioners, Ghana (since 2019), she is known for aligning human capital strategy with institutional goals, driving process excellence, and fostering environments where people and organisations thrive together.
✉️ [email protected]📞 Ext. 209

Success Conditions
Measurable milestones · Dec 2026 – Dec 2028

Guiding Principles

🏆
GH¢4.14m
Phase 1 Rationalisation
437 excess credits eliminated · 29 programmes · 68% of surplus
🔀
Programme Mergers
Strategy 2 · Pathway-based degrees · Fewer titles, stronger brands, bigger cohorts

Overview

Multiple small-cohort programmes are being merged into pathway-based degrees — creating fewer, stronger programme brands with larger, more viable cohorts. This reduces timetabling fragmentation while preserving academic diversity through elective specialisation tracks.

Design Mergers (SADe)

Graphic Design Real Estate + industry pathways → Bachelor of Design (BDes)

Shared studio foundations · 6 industry pathway tracks · single accreditation base

Theology & Ministry Mergers (FASS)

BA Theology Family Counselling Church Administration Christian Education Biblical Studies Leadership → Pathway-based Ministry degree

Public Health Consolidation

Multiple MPH variants Health Policy stream → Unified MPH (single Miotso stream)

Phase Timeline

Phase 1 · March–Sept 2026
✦ Admission freezes (March 2026)✦ Teach-out plans lodged✦ Campus specialisation (Sept 2026)✦ First CU MBA + merged cohorts enrolled
Phase 2 · Oct 2026–Sept 2027
✦ Level 400 teach-outs completed✦ 10–15% Year 1 savings realised✦ 58% micro-class reduction
Phase 3 · Oct 2027–Dec 2028
✦ Full optimisation achieved✦ All 10 success conditions met✦ Formal declaration to Academic Board
Central University · 2023–2028 · Year 3 of 5 · VC Roadmap 2025–2029

LEGEND: 🟢 G = On track (≤5% variance) 🟡 A = At risk (6–20% variance) 🔴 R = Off track (>20% or missed) — = Not yet due / data pending—————————
DIRECTOR'S WORKFLOW: (1) Receive monthly updates from school focal persons (2) Enter RAG in school tabs (3) Copy RAG summary here (4) Any RED = immediate escalation (5) ≥3 AMBER in one school = Dean review meeting—————————
Legend:  🟢 G = On track (≤5% variance)  🟡 A = At risk (6–20%)  🔴 R = Off track (>20% or missed)  — = Not yet due

THRUST 3: FINANCIAL SUSTAINABILITY

All schools are expected to diversify revenue through short courses and CPD, establish grants acquisition processes, engage the Alumni Advisory Board, and contribute to academic rationalisation.

Amber — Early Stage
⚠ Non-tuition revenue still minimal institution-wide. All schools to submit IGF design plans and short course proposals to Finance within 45 days.

Key Targets — T3

YearKey Targets
2026All schools: at least 1 short course/CPD designed; Grants Acquisition Committees established
20272–4 professional programmes admitted; first CPD revenue generated institution-wide
2028All programmes financially viable or rationalised; meaningful non-tuition contribution

2 short courses/certificate programmes launched

FASS · Faculty of
DOSS: 5 certificate programmes to run; DCMS: semester-based certificates, open days, digital marketing; DEDS: new undergraduate/postgraduate programmes launched
— Baseline period

— Baseline period

Implementation Timeline

Year 1 Focus
✓
Feb 2026
Briefing Paper Submitted
Phase 2 plan presented to VC — all 4 strategies, school summaries, financial impact, and draft policies.
●
Q1–Q2 2026
Academic Board Approvals
9 resolutions for approval — closures, merged degree titles, canonical courses, delivery model, class-size rules.
○
2026/27 Intake
Closures & Mergers Effective
No new intake into 14 closed programmes. First BDes and unified BThM cohorts launched.
○
Nov 2026
Year 1 Teach-Out Complete
All final-year affected students taught out. 58% micro-class reduction achieved.
○
Dec 2028
Full Phase 2 Completion
GH¢4.4–4.9m annual savings realised. All 10 success conditions met. Combined P1+P2 savings: GH¢9.34m/yr.

Phase 2 Total: GH¢4.4–4.9 million per year · Combined with Phase 1 (GH¢4.14m): GH¢9.34 million in total annual recurring savings · Year-1 target: 50% of financial benefit achievable immediately through closure of non-viable programmes and teach-out of Level 400 micro-classes.

🚫
Strategy Title
Sub-heading

Enrolment Efficiency Analysis

Class-level efficiency review · All schools, campuses & streams · February 2026 · Confidential — Internal Management

📊 February 2026 Snapshot
9335
Total Students
365
Total Classes
65%
Classes <10 Students
25.6
Avg Class Size
8.9
Avg CBS Class
⚠️
Headline Finding
Nearly 65% of all classes — 235 out of 365 — have fewer than 10 students. Only 35 classes (9.6%) reach the 100+ threshold where genuine economies of scale operate. A handful of flagship programmes teach hundreds efficiently while the majority of classes are economically indefensible.

Class Size Distribution — 365 Classes

18

19
19 · 5.2%

35
35 · 9.6%
UG Classes (L100–400)
Threshold: 20 students
236 total
165
Below Min
70% of UG classes
71

Only 30% comply
PG Classes (L500–900)
Threshold: 15 students
114 total
102
Below Min
89.5% of PG classes
12
At or Above
Only 10.5% comply
🔴 Most Severe Problem: Postgraduate Programmes
89.5% of all PG classes fall below the 15-student minimum viable threshold. This sits almost entirely in CBS and FASS and is the single biggest structural problem in the institution.

Campus Efficiency Analysis

February 2026
CampusStudents% of TotalClassesAvg Class SizeVerdictNote
Miotso 6,783 72.9% 165 41.1 ✓ Efficient Carries 73% of students. The university's viable core.
Christ Temple 1,930 20.7% 159 16.0 ⚠ Critical Weekend/evening fragmentation. Single biggest efficiency drain.
Kumasi 591 6.3% 37 16.0 ~ Marginal Viable only for Law. Should be rationalised to LLB Weekend.

Details ▾
ProgrammeStudentsClassesAvg/ClassRating
LLB Weekend1,22110122.1Efficient
LLB6791067.9Efficient
LLB Evening112618.3Poor
Dip Law25212.5Poor
LLB Weekend (122/class) and LLB (68/class) are the financial anchors of the university. LLB Evening is a legacy stream on teach-out — do not grow. Consider absorbing Dip Law students into the LLB mainstream.
School of Pharmacy
1,223 students · 14 classes
✓ Efficient87.4
1,223
Students
14
Classes
87.4
Avg/Class
⭐ PharmD is the most efficient programme in CU
Details ▾
ProgrammeStudentsClassesAvg/ClassRating
PharmD1,0566176.0Efficient
PharmD Top-Up137622.8Marginal
BPharm321.5Critical
PharmD at 176 students per class is the gold standard — the most efficient programme in the institution. BPharm is a ghost: 3 students, 2 classes. Close immediately and migrate students into PharmD or Top-Up.
School of Nursing & Midwifery
1,175 students · 10 classes
✓ Efficient117.5
1,175
Students
10
Classes
117.5
Avg/Class
🏆 Best-in-class average across the university
Details ▾
ProgrammeStudentsClassesAvg/ClassRating
BSc Nursing — Generics8755174.4Efficient
BSc Nursing — Professionals (WE)289396.3Efficient
Nursing — Professionals Afternoon221.0Critical
Nursing Generics (174/class) rivals PharmD as the most efficient programme in the institution. Professionals Weekend (97/class) is equally impressive — this proves what weekend streams can achieve at scale. The Afternoon stream (2 students, 2 classes) is an administrative error. Close immediately.
School of Engineering & Technology
936 students · 12 classes
✓ Efficient78.0
936
Students
12
Classes
78.0
Avg/Class
✅ Zero weekend/multi-campus fragmentation
Details ▾
ProgrammeStudentsClassesAvg/ClassRating
BSc Information Technology4144103.5Efficient
BSc Computer Science369492.3Efficient
BSc Civil Engineering118429.5Adequate
IT and CS are the two most efficient UG degree programmes outside the professional schools. Every class is Miotso Morning — zero weekend, evening, or multi-campus fragmentation. This is the model all schools should replicate.
School of Medical Sciences
718 students · 13 classes
~ Adequate55.2
718
Students
13

55.2
Avg/Class
⚠️ MPH sub-programmes need urgent action
Details ▾
ProgrammeStudentsClassesAvg/ClassRating
B. Public Health332655.3Efficient
Physician Assistantship156352.0Efficient
Master of Public Health1744.2Critical
MPH (18-month)717.0Critical
Public Health and PA are solid at 55+ per class. MPH is a disaster — 17 students across 4 classes at 4.2 average. The 18-month variant (7 students) should close. Standard MPH needs consolidation to a single stream or closure. Kumasi Public Health (7 students) should transfer to Miotso.
ATHE Diplomas
625 students · 16 classes

39.1
Avg/Class
💎 Hidden efficiency gem — invest in marketing
Details ▾
ProgrammeStudentsClassesAvg/ClassRating
Dip in Info & Digital Tech1091109.0Efficient
Dip in Health & Social Care97197.0Efficient
Dip in Law141347.3Adequate
Dip in Business & Mgt114338.3Adequate
Dip in Business80326.7Marginal
Dip in Mgt (Operations)422.5Critical
Dip in IT (109/class) and Dip in Health (97/class) are two of the most efficient single-class programmes in the entire institution. These diplomas deserve investment and marketing. Dip in Management Operations (4 students, 2 classes) should be closed immediately.
School of Architecture & Design
547 students · 21 classes

⚠️ Average masks collapse without Architecture
Details ▾
ProgrammeStudentsClassesAvg/ClassRating
B. Architecture417583.4Efficient
BSc Real Estate60411.0Poor
BSc Graphic Design3949.8Poor
BSc Interior Design2035.7Critical
BSc Planning723.5Critical
BSc Fashion Design1032.7Critical
BSc Landscape Design111.0Critical
BSc Architecture (legacy)111.0Critical
B. Architecture (83/class) is the sole reason SADe's average looks acceptable. Remove it and the school average collapses to 6.5 — critical territory. Fashion Design, Interior Design, and Planning are the BDes merger targets — this data confirms the merger is not just desirable, it is urgent. Landscape Design (1 student) and legacy Architecture (1 student) should close immediately.

782 students · 75 classes — Most fragmented school in the university

4 viable programmes: MA Sacred Ministry, BA Comm Studies, BSc Psychology, B. Divinity — protect these. MPhil Religious Studies (4 students, 4 classes, 1.0 avg) is not a programme cohort — it is 4 individuals enrolled in isolation. BA Theology (34 students, 9 classes, 3.8 avg) is scattered across 4 campuses and 2 streams. Immediate consolidation required across FASS.
Central Business School (CBS)
1,383 students · 155 classes — Most critically inefficient school · Accounts for 46.3% of all classes, only 14.6% of students

🔴 CBS alone generates more admin burden than the rest of the university combined

BSc Admin (HRM)3301620.6Marginal

MBA & Postgraduate Programmes
ProgrammeStudentsClassesAvgRating
MBA General Management5286.5Critical

MSc Accounting30310.0Poor
MBA HRM2864.7Critical

MBA Finance (18m)1853.8Critical
MBA HRM (18m)1836.0Critical
MBA Marketing1553.2Critical
MBA Project Mgt (18m)1543.8Critical
MBA Marketing (18m)522.5Critical
MBA Accounting (18m)422.0Critical
EM Leadership & Governance941.8Critical
The CBS MBA is the clearest single example of programme fragmentation in the institution. 8 MBA variants + 18-month versions = 222 students in 50 classes at 4.4/class average. As a single consolidated CU MBA they would average 22/class — viable. The 4 weekend UG variants (68 students, 37 classes, avg 5.4) cost more in staff time and overhead than they generate. BSc Banking & Finance Weekend (9 students, 7 classes, 1.3 avg) must close immediately.

Key Conclusions & Recommended Actions

#FindingImplication
1Two-tier operationLaw, Pharmacy, Nursing, Engineering, ATHE average 42–117/class. CBS, FASS, SADe average 8–10/class. The efficient schools are subsidising the rest.
2CBS is most urgent169 classes, 1,307 students, avg 7.7/class. CBS alone generates more admin burden, timetabling complexity, and staffing cost than the rest of the university combined.
3Christ Temple is structurally broken112 classes, 1,793 students, avg 16.0/class. Almost every problem class is a Christ Temple evening or weekend section. Should only deliver consolidated, viable programmes.
4PG programmes non-viable89.5% of all PG classes fall below the 15-student minimum. This is system-wide. MBA merger and unified PG delivery are essential — not optional.
5Two-speed university is unsustainableTop 5 programmes (PharmD, Nursing, LLB WE, IT, CS) teach 3,932 students in 29 classes. Bottom 100 programmes teach 1,200 students in 235 classes. This is the core financial problem.

🤝 Ecoligo Partnership

Since Nov 2018
☀️
Solar-as-a-Service Agreement
Ecoligo GmbH (Germany) supplies, installs, and operates the solar system. CU pays for energy consumed at a fixed rate — zero capital outlay.
Agreement Signed26 November 2018
Total System Capacity401.28 kWp
Solar Panels1,216 × Yingli Solar 330Wp
Inverters6 × SMA STP60
Phase 1 (Admin + Academic)276 kWp
Phase 2 (Hostels)125 kWp
⚠️ Performance ratios in Hostels & Academic arrays have declined significantly in 2024–25. A maintenance review with Ecoligo is recommended to restore output to 2023 levels.

⚠ Total Opportunity: GH₵ 338,061 / year
Restoring Hostel and Academic arrays to 2023 performance levels would recover GH₵338K+ annually in avoided ECG costs. An urgent maintenance audit with Ecoligo is recommended.

Solar Availability Collapse vs ECG Bill Rise

Inverse Correlation
Total ECG Spend (bars)
Total Solar Generation kWh (bars)
Avg System Availability % (line)

As campus-wide solar availability drops (purple line), grid dependency — and ECG bills — rise proportionally. The Admin array's stability is the only reason admin bills are falling.

👥 RACI Framework
Roles & responsibilities for M&E implementation · 16 stakeholders
StakeholderM&E ResponsibilitiesRole
PSSP DirectorOverall M&E coordination; survey design & administration; quarterly and annual reports; Governing Council presentationsResponsible
Vice-ChancellorStrategic oversight; endorsing M&E reports; acting on critical findings; championing VC Roadmap implementationAccountable
Finance DirectorateFinancial data collection; budget execution, liquidity, IGF, endowment updates; quarterly management accountsResponsible
HR DirectorateStaff retention, CPD attendance, appraisal completion, promotion records; D&I metrics; pay review dataResponsible
CQAD DirectorAccreditation status updates; QA data; programme compliance; academic standards oversightResponsible
Research Mgt Office NEWResearch grant tracking; publications monitoring; Scopus/WoS citation data; IP logResponsible
ICT DirectorateInfrastructure audit data; LMS usage statistics; network coverage reports; digital administration metricsResponsible
Facilities DirectorateSafety audit data; infrastructure improvement tracking; accommodation development milestonesResponsible
Deans & DirectorsSchool/directorate progress reports; academic delivery data; faculty development trackingResponsible
Dean of StudentsStudent welfare data; co-curricular activity metrics; hostel and safety incident reportsResponsible
Media Affairs / PRSocial media analytics; media mentions; CU Radio output; brand perception surveysResponsible
Alumni OfficeAlumni chapter data; engagement rates; financial/non-financial contributions; alumni giving recordsResponsible
Academic BoardReview and validate M&E reports; approve corrective actions; strategic input and oversightConsulted
Governing CouncilReceive and endorse annual M&E reports; governance oversight; review end-term evaluationInformed
SRCFacilitate student surveys; validate satisfaction data; community voice in M&E findingsConsulted
External EvaluatorConduct independent mid-term (2026) and end-term (2028) assessments; validate data; produce evaluation reportsResponsible (evals)
⚠️ M&E Risk Register
11 identified risks · Likelihood · Impact · Mitigation strategies
RiskLikelihoodImpactOverallMitigation
Low survey response rates (2.9% baseline)HIGHHIGH● CRITICALIncentives; multi-channel (OSIS mandatory, WhatsApp); SRC champions; shorten survey to ≤15 min
Staff reluctance to report honestlyHIGHHIGH● CRITICALFull anonymity; external survey platform; share results transparently with all staff
Financial constraints limiting infrastructureHIGHHIGH● CRITICALM&E to flag priority gaps; link findings to budget cycle; advocate for earmarked infrastructure budget
Inconsistent data reporting from DirectoratesMEDHIGH● HIGHStandardised templates; quarterly reminders; PSSP follow-up protocol
Research Management Office not establishedMEDHIGH● HIGHPSSP tracks Obj. 1.4 milestones; interim coordination by SGSR; escalate to VC if delayed past Q2 2026
M&E findings not acted upon by managementMEDHIGH● HIGHLink findings formally to budget decisions; present to Governing Council annually; VC sign-off
VC Roadmap KPIs not tracked separatelyMEDMED● MEDIUMDual tracking in dashboards; amber highlighting in all reports; dedicated VC Roadmap scorecard
CQAD capacity constraintsMEDMED● MEDIUMHire additional CQAD staff; streamline reporting processes; accreditation pipeline tracker
High turnover affecting trend comparisonLOWMED● MEDIUMTrack cohort-level data; separate new vs. continuing respondent data in reporting
Data quality issues (self-reporting bias)MEDMED● MEDIUMTriangulate with admin data; FGDs to validate; minimum completion threshold before analysis
VC Roadmap 2029 targets exceed Plan end-dateLOWMED● LOWAgree bridge reporting with Governing Council; build 2029 targets into next Strategic Plan
💰 Indicative M&E Budget 2025–2028
~GHS 131,000/year · Subject to Finance Directorate approval
M&E ActivityEst. Cost (GHS)FrequencyAnnual Cost
Student, Staff & Employer Satisfaction Surveys20,000Annual20,000
Survey incentives (airtime/vouchers)10,000Annual10,000
Focus Group Discussions (semi-annual)8,000Semi-annual16,000
Key Informant Interviews5,000Annual5,000
M&E software/platform & dashboard licences10,000Annual10,000
Quarterly & Annual Report production12,000Annual12,000
VC Roadmap Annual Scorecard production NEW5,000Annual5,000
Annual Learning Forum8,000Annual8,000
Research publications & grants monitoring NEW6,000Annual6,000
Media & digital analytics tools NEW4,000Annual4,000
Mid-Term External Evaluation (2026)60,000Once15,000 (amortised)
End-Term External Evaluation (2028)80,000Once20,000 (amortised)
PSSP staff coordination costsStaff costs absorbedOngoingWithin PSSP budget
TOTAL INDICATIVE ANNUAL M&E BUDGET~GHS 131,000
Final allocations subject to Finance Directorate approval and inclusion in the Annual Budget. PSSP staff coordination costs absorbed within PSSP operating budget.
